# The 7 Principles of Xeriscape Gardens

![](https://sensorex.com/wp-content/smush-webp/2020/12/succulent-804988_1280-e1608612990441.jpg.webp)

Table of Contents

When you’re searching for the right garden to build, there are many options available to you. Among the most beautiful and [environmentally friendly gardens](https://sensorex.com/blog/2020/01/28/water-quality-association/) that you can create is a xeriscape garden, which essentially **refers to creating a garden of plants that require very little water**. In dryer climates, xeriscape gardens have become increasingly popular. However, it’s important for gardeners to choose to create a xeriscape garden even in wetter climates. By building a garden with dry plants, you will be able to conserve water by reducing the water that you use when growing the plants. **Since water has become relatively scarce, doing your part to [conserve water](https://sensorex.com/2022/08/30/implications-reclaimed-water-use-agriculture/) can be great for the environment.**  
In Latin, xeriscape can be separated into the two words of xero and scape. The word xero means dry, while the word scape refers to a view or landscape. When used correctly, xeriscape gardening can [reduce the use of landscape water](https://sensorex.com/blog/2020/03/10/municipal-water-treatment-applications/) by *anywhere from 50-75 percent*. The types of plants that are considered to be xeriscape friendly include various types of grasses, cacti, and succulent plants, the latter of which extend to echeveria, aloe, and sempervivum. These plants are great for this type of garden because they don’t require much water and can live without issue in dryer climates. This article provides you with seven of the guiding principles that you should adhere to as you attempt to build a xeriscape garden.

**Key Takeaways:**

## The Principles Behind Xeriscaping

Xeriscape gardens are simple to create as long as you follow the right principles of xeriscaping. When done correctly, your xeriscape garden will be wholly unique from any other in appearance. While there are a wide range of different plants that can be grown in a xeriscape garden, the **seven following principles serve as guidelines that will help you build a xeriscape garden** without making a mistake.

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='1024'%20height='682'%20viewBox='0%200%201024%20682'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://sensorex.com/wp-content/smush-webp/2023/01/rake-2915742_1280-1024x682.jpg.webp)

### 1. Water Conservation

The first and likely most important principle of xeriscape gardens is water conservation. When designing this type of garden, **it’s important to look for methods that you can use to reduce water use and increase the amount of natural precipitation** that your plants receive. You can visit some local nurseries to gain a better idea of the types of drought-resistant plants that can be purchased near your home. You should then draw a top-down view of your property with graph paper, which will serve as the plan for your xeriscape garden.  
First, you’ll want to plot the entire land on your property, which you can do by marking east, west, north, and south on the graph paper. From here, make note of all limiting features that could obstruct your garden, which include everything from walkways to trees. All areas of shade and sun should be marked on the paper, which will allow you to determine water needs for the plants that you grow. Make sure that you also look at the drainage patterns and contours of the land while creating the plan for the garden. The contours can be turned into terraces that lessen soil erosion.  
You should then focus on how you plan to use every area of the plot. **Define all areas of dining, play, seating, and walkways**. If you have areas that you are keeping as standard turf, these areas should be simple to mow. Try to avoid any narrow strips of turf.

### 2. Soil Improvement

Another important principle of xeriscape gardens is soil improvement. The soil that you use in your xeriscape garden **should be able to store water and drain very quickly at the same time**, which isn’t always an easy balance to strike. Both of these steps can be done at the same time by increasing the total amount of organic material that’s situated in the soil. This organic material should be kept well aerated. *Likely the top organic additive is compost*, which is ideal for nearly all xeriscape plants. While cacti and succulents require very little water, they do need to be placed in a lean soil.  
If you’re finding it difficult to get the right balance, you might need to test your soil, which can be done with a [home test kit](https://sensorex.com/blog/2020/08/31/water-ph-test-kit/) or similar type of sensor. This kit will provide you with readings on the pH levels of your soil. If these levels are too high and provide you with alkaline readings of 7.5 or above, the plants could be low in phosphorus, which they require for energy. You can increase phosphorus levels by adding rock phosphate and bone meal.

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='1024'%20height='637'%20viewBox='0%200%201024%20637'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://sensorex.com/wp-content/smush-webp/2023/01/succulents-1030982_1920-1024x637.jpg.webp)

### 3. Limited Turf Area

The next principle of a xeriscape garden is to have limited turf areas around your home. While the amount of turf should be reduced as much as possible, it’s still recommended that you keep some turf areas for visual appeal and open space. If you’re currently planting new turf, **it’s a good idea to use a species of turf that requires less water**. Turf is a great option for your landscaping because it prevents erosion and [reduces water runoff](https://sensorex.com/blog/2020/02/04/how-does-groundwater-become-contaminated/).

### 4. Use Plants Native to Your Region

When creating a xeriscape garden, it’s very important that you use plants that are native to your specific region, which **ensures that these plants are able to survive without issue once planted**. You should use drought-resistant plants with small and thick leaves, which are two defining characteristics of plants that don’t require an ample amount of water. Some of the plants that are native to California include bush anemone, California bush sunflower, and woolly blue curls.  
**You should also obtain plants that only need a small amount of water for the hot and dry areas of your yard**. When growing plants along walls or slopes that face to the east or north, you can select plants that require a decent amount of water. Keep in mind that trees are able to lessen evaporation by shading the soil of your garden and by blocking the wind. If you want to use less water with these plants, you might want to consider obtaining a [water pressure regulator](https://sensorex.com/blog/2020/11/02/water-pressure-regulators-function/), which will keep pressure low when placed at the right setting.

### 5. Mulch

Yet another main principle of xeriscape gardens is mulch, **which is meant to cover the soil in your garden**. Mulch can consist of anything from pine needles and wood chips to compost and leaves. Keep in mind that mulch is able to retain the moisture levels and temperature of soil. *It can also block out weeds and prevent soil erosion from occurring*. If you use organic mulch, this mulch will eventually become integrated into the soil, which means that additional mulch will need to be added at a later time. Make sure that the mulch you lay down is at least several inches in thickness with no spots of bare soil.

### 6. Irrigate

Irrigation is essential for any garden and can be the difference between a xeriscape garden and a standard garden. Your main goal should be to conserve as much water as possible, which is why it’s important that you avoid overwatering issues. Likely the **top two methods to use when irrigating your garden include drip-irrigation systems and soaker hoses**, both of which are efficient and are designed with water conservation in mind.  
These methods of irrigation send water to the base of all of the plants in your garden, which lessens the amount of moisture that you lose with evaporation. If you plant an ample amount of succulents in your garden, *these plants only require watering every 2-3 weeks*. When conserving water, make sure that you irrigate your plants less frequently than you normally would.

### 7. Maintenance

Another key principle and top benefit of xeriscape gardens is that these gardens require very little maintenance. However, **it’s still highly important that you maintain your landscape if you want to keep the garden and the plants within in good condition**. While keeping weeds at bay will be necessary for these gardens, placing a thicker layer of mulch over the soil should reduce the amount of weeds that grow.  
It’s also essential that your turf areas aren’t cut to be too short. When this occurs, moisture isn’t properly retained. Make sure that you avoid over-fertilizing as well, which results from placing too much fertilizer in the soil. This can cause plants to die more rapidly than they otherwise would.

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='1024'%20height='752'%20viewBox='0%200%201024%20752'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://sensorex.com/wp-content/smush-webp/2023/01/boris-debusscher-9Rtp9uvTs3Q-unsplash-1024x752.jpg.webp)

## Overall Benefits of Xeriscape Gardening

Xeriscape gardening is a wonderful form of gardening that can help you conserve water and protect the environment. The main benefits of xeriscape gardening include:

A good xeriscape garden comes with an array of benefits that can’t be found with other types of gardens. Whether you want to conserve water and protect the environment or live in a climate that doesn’t facilitate other garden types, **creating a xeriscape garden gives you the ability to build a garden that matches your personality and is highly versatile.**

# What Are the Benefits of Xeriscaping in Landscaping?

Xeriscaping offers several benefits in [landscaping](https://mdlandscape.ca/property-maintenance), primarily by reducing water usage and maintenance needs. This approach involves using drought-tolerant plants and efficient irrigation methods, which help conserve water and lower utility bills. Xeriscaping also minimizes the need for fertilizers and pesticides, making it an eco-friendly choice. By selecting plants that are well-suited to the local climate, xeriscaping creates a resilient and low-maintenance garden that thrives with minimal intervention. Additionally, this landscaping method can enhance the aesthetic appeal of outdoor spaces while contributing to overall environmental sustainability.

At [MD Landscape & Design](https://www.google.com/maps?cid=3923352828381212556), we specialize in transforming outdoor spaces in Thunder Bay and beyond, and we’re passionate about incorporating xeriscaping techniques to enhance both beauty and functionality in landscaping. Let’s explore the multifaceted benefits of xeriscaping and how it can revolutionize your outdoor space.

**Key Takeaways:**

**Water Conservation:** Reduces landscape water usage by 50-75%, conserving precious water resources.

**Cost Savings:** Can lower water bills by up to 80% and reduce maintenance costs by 87%.

**Low Maintenance:** Requires minimal mowing, watering, and pruning.

**Increased Property Value:** Can boost property values by 14%.

**Reduced Pollution:** Minimizes the need for gas-powered lawn mowers and chemical fertilizers.

**Improved Aesthetics:** Offers a vibrant, thriving landscape with year-round color and interest.

## **Benefits Of Sustainable Landscaping**

### **Water Conservation**

Sustainable landscaping methods are designed to conserve water effectively. By implementing these practices, you can significantly reduce the amount of water used for maintaining your landscape. Here are the key aspects:

**Reduces Landscape Water Usage**: Lowers water consumption by 50-75%, helping to conserve precious water resources.

**Efficient Irrigation**: Utilizes water-efficient irrigation systems that target plant roots more effectively.

**Drought-Resistant Plants**: Incorporates plants that require less water, reducing overall irrigation needs.

### **Cost Savings**

One of the major benefits of sustainable landscaping is the reduction in costs associated with water and maintenance. This approach provides significant financial savings over time. Key points include:

**Lower Water Bills**: Can reduce water expenses by up to 80%, thanks to decreased water usage.

**Reduced Maintenance Costs**: Cuts maintenance expenses by up to 87%, due to less frequent mowing and pruning.

**Economic Advantages**: Saves money on garden upkeep and water resources, providing long-term financial benefits.

### **Low Maintenance**

Sustainable landscaping simplifies garden care, requiring less frequent attention compared to traditional methods. This results in lower maintenance efforts. Here’s how:

**Minimal Mowing**: Requires less frequent mowing, thanks to the use of groundcovers and low-growth plants.

**Reduced Watering**: Utilizes drought-tolerant plants that need less water, reducing the need for frequent irrigation.

**Less Pruning**: Chooses plants that grow more slowly or have natural pest resistance, minimizing pruning needs.

### **Increased Property Value**

Investing in sustainable landscaping can enhance your property’s market value, making it more attractive to potential buyers. Important aspects include:

**Boosts Property Values**: Can increase property values by up to 14%, reflecting the overall appeal and sustainability of your landscape.

**Attractive to Buyers**: Eco-friendly features and well-maintained landscapes are often desirable traits for homebuyers.

**Enhanced Curb Appeal**: Improves the visual attractiveness of your property, contributing to higher market value.

### **Reduced Pollution**

Sustainable landscaping reduces the environmental impact associated with traditional garden maintenance practices. Key benefits include:

**Minimizes Gas-Powered Equipment**: Reduces reliance on gas-powered lawn mowers, cutting down on air pollution.

**Lower Chemical Usage**: Decreases the need for chemical fertilizers and pesticides, reducing soil and water pollution.

**Eco-Friendly Practices**: Promotes practices that are less harmful to the environment and local wildlife.

### **Improved Aesthetics**

Sustainable landscaping can enhance the beauty of your outdoor space, offering a visually appealing and functional environment. Key points include:

**Year-Round Color**: Selects plants that provide vibrant color and interest throughout all seasons.

**Thriving Landscape**: Creates a lush and healthy garden with minimal effort, enhancing the overall aesthetic appeal.

**Eco-Friendly Design**: Incorporates design elements that are both beautiful and environmentally responsible.

## **Smart Landscaping Strategies**

### **Water-Efficient Landscaping Techniques**

Water-efficient landscaping is designed to minimize water use while ensuring your garden remains lush and attractive. This approach incorporates various strategies to conserve water and reduce the environmental footprint of traditional landscaping practices. By focusing on drought-tolerant plants, efficient irrigation systems, and effective mulching techniques, homeowners can create beautiful, water-smart gardens. This not only helps in areas facing water scarcity but also contributes to sustainable living by lowering water bills and reducing overall maintenance.

**Drought-Tolerant Plants:** Choose plants that thrive in low-water conditions, reducing the need for frequent irrigation.

**Efficient Irrigation Systems:** Implement drip irrigation or soaker hoses to deliver water directly to plant roots.

**Mulching:** Use organic mulch to retain soil moisture and reduce evaporation.

### **Benefits of Native Plants in Landscaping**

Native plants are well-suited to local climate and soil conditions, making them an ideal choice for sustainable landscaping. These plants require less water, fertilizer, and pesticides compared to non-native varieties. They also support local wildlife, such as bees and butterflies, contributing to biodiversity. By incorporating native plants into your garden, you enhance its resilience to pests and diseases, reduce maintenance needs, and create a more balanced ecosystem. Embracing native plants not only benefits the environment but also ensures a vibrant, low-maintenance landscape.

**Lower Maintenance:** Native plants require less care and resources, making them easier to manage.

**Environmental Benefits:** Support local wildlife and promote biodiversity.

**Pest and Disease Resistance:** Native plants are naturally resistant to local pests and diseases.

### **Cost-Effective Landscaping Solutions**

Cost-effective landscaping focuses on achieving value without overspending. This involves selecting durable and low-maintenance materials and implementing efficient design choices. By using materials like gravel or native stone and incorporating efficient irrigation systems, homeowners can create beautiful outdoor spaces that minimize long-term expenses. This approach is ideal for those who want to create stunning landscapes on a budget while maintaining high standards of quality and sustainability.

**Durable Materials:** Opt for long-lasting materials like gravel or native stone to reduce replacement costs.

**Efficient Irrigation:** Implement water-saving irrigation systems to lower water bills.

**Low-Maintenance Designs:** Choose designs that require minimal upkeep to save on maintenance costs.

## **FAQs**

**What is xeriscaping?** Xeriscaping is a landscaping approach designed to reduce water usage and maintenance by incorporating drought-tolerant plants and efficient design principles. It focuses on creating sustainable and low-water-use landscapes.

**How much can xeriscaping reduce water usage?** Xeriscaping can cut landscape water usage by 50-75%, depending on the plants used and the local climate.

**Does xeriscaping require a lot of maintenance?** No, xeriscaping is low-maintenance as it uses native plants that are adapted to the local climate, reducing the need for frequent watering, mowing, and pruning.

**Can xeriscaping increase my property value?** Yes, xeriscaping can increase property values by up to 14% due to its appealing, drought-tolerant design and reduced maintenance requirements.

**How does xeriscaping reduce pollution?** Xeriscaping minimizes the need for gas-powered lawn mowers and eliminates the use of chemical fertilizers and pesticides, leading to a reduction in environmental pollution.

## **Embrace Sustainable Elegance with MD Landscape & Design**

Xeriscaping offers a multitude of benefits that make it an attractive option for modern landscaping. From substantial water savings to reduced maintenance and enhanced property value, the advantages are clear. At MD Landscape & Design, we are dedicated to helping you create a stunning and sustainable outdoor space in Thunder Bay. By integrating xeriscaping principles, we ensure your landscape is both beautiful and environmentally friendly. 

Don’t wait to enhance your garden with innovative landscaping solutions. [Contact us](https://mdlandscape.ca/contact-us) now to start your journey towards a greener, more efficient outdoor space in Thunder Bay. Let’s make your landscaping dreams a reality!

Xeriscaping 101: How to Grow a Vibrant Garden with Less Water
=============================================================

Primary Image

![Image 3: Perennial flowers for a dry garden.](https://www.almanac.com/sites/default/files/styles/or/public/image_nodes/shutterstock_1443722447.jpg?itok=mM8tSYd0)

Caption

Not enough rain? Select the right plants and enjoy a colorful, vibrant garden!

Photo Credit

Kathryn Roach/Shutterstock

Your easy guide to watering less without sacrificing beauty.
------------------------------------------------------------

[Samantha Johnson](https://www.almanac.com/author/samantha-johnson)

July 11, 2025

[![Image 4: Print Friendly and PDF](https://www.almanac.com/themes/almanaco/images/print-icon.png)](https://www.printfriendly.com/ "Printer Friendly and PDF")

[Share](https://www.addtoany.com/share#url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.almanac.com%2Fxeriscaping-101-how-grow-vibrant-garden-less-water&title=Xeriscaping%20101%3A%20How%20to%20Grow%20a%20Vibrant%20Garden%20with%20Less%20Water)[Facebook](https://www.almanac.com/#facebook)[Email](https://www.almanac.com/#email)

For daily wit & wisdom, sign up for the Almanac newsletter.

Email Address 

 No content available. 

Body

Droughts, watering bans, rising water bills—these days, watering your thirsty yard is more of a challenge. That’s where xeriscaping comes in! Our beginner-friendly guide will walk you through the basics of xeriscaping, from choosing the right plants to soil preparation and maintenance, so you can enjoy a beautiful garden that thrives with less water.

What is Xeriscaping?
--------------------

Xeriscaping — from the Greek word _xeros_, meaning “dry,” combined with “landscaping” — is a commonsense approach to gardening that uses less water. It’s a method designed to [work in harmony with your local climate](https://www.almanac.com/what-are-plant-hardiness-zones), creating an attractive, eco-friendly yard or garden that utilizes plants requiring minimal water and care.

![Image 5: xeriscape garden with colorful perennials, and ground cover](https://www.almanac.com/sites/default/files/images/xeriscape%202%20Kathryn%20Roach%20shutterstock.jpg)

A water-wise wonderland! This vibrant xeriscape proves you don’t need much water to grow a garden full of color and life. Photo credit: Kathryn Roach

Today, Mother Nature can’t always be counted on to provide the [inch of water per week](https://www.canr.msu.edu/news/watering-strategies-to-keep-a-garden-productive) most plants need to thrive. That might not sound like much, but one inch of rain equals about 62 gallons of water per 100 square feet of garden space! In dry regions, relying on traditional turfgrass or thirsty garden plants can quickly become unsustainable.

Read Next
---------

*   [21 Inspiring Rock Garden Ideas: Low-Maintenance, Modern, and More](https://www.almanac.com/21-inspiring-rock-garden-ideas-low-maintenance-modern-and-more) 
*   [Beat the Heat: Top Drought-Tolerant Plants for a Resilient Garden](https://www.almanac.com/beat-heat-top-drought-tolerant-plants-resilient-garden) 
*   [12 Best Types of Grass for Your Lawn](https://www.almanac.com/12-best-types-of-grass) 

But xeriscaping isn’t just for dry climates—it’s a gardening philosophy for anyone who wants to work with nature, not against it. Whether you’re looking to [replace your lawn](https://www.almanac.com/grass-alternatives-lawn-replacements) or simply reduce watering, xeriscaping is about a low-maintenance approach to gardening that prioritizes healthy plants that aren’t water hogs.

Rather than completely removing [turfgrass](https://www.almanac.com/12-best-types-of-grass) and replacing it with rocks, xeriscaping finds a balanced middle ground. A well-designed xeriscape might include some turfgrass or decorative stones alongside drought-tolerant plants and shrubs that thrive on the water naturally available in your environment.

![Image 6: xeriscape garden in full bloom with blue and red flowers](https://www.almanac.com/sites/default/files/images/xeriscape-garden-shutterstock_1745535287.jpg)

_Fill your xeriscape garden with plenty of visual interest and pops of color. Credit: Kathryn Roach_

Benefits of Xeriscaping
-----------------------

Xeriscaping, how do we love thee? Let us count the ways…

### 1. Water Conservation

Originally designed to conserve water in the dry U.S. Southwest, xeriscaping reduces water use by 50 percent or more. Its principles can be applied anywhere—even in areas with abundant rainfall—to create a naturally beautiful environment that requires a minimum of care and little, if any, irrigation. By choosing plants that naturally thrive in your local rainfall, xeriscaping helps you cut back significantly on watering. You’ll still need to water sometimes, but less often—and when you do, soak the soil deeply to encourage strong roots that can find water on their own.

### 2. Reduced Maintenance

Unlike [high-maintenance plants like tomatoes](https://www.almanac.com/plant/tomatoes), native and drought-tolerant plants grow happily with little fuss. They’re tough, adapted to your environment, and need less watering, weeding, and feeding—meaning less work for you. Plus, you can still have a colorful, vibrant garden that suits your style.

![Image 7: pink-purple flowers of thyme plant](https://www.almanac.com/sites/default/files/images/thyme-flowers-ss.jpg)

Thyme is a Mediterranean herb that is drought-friendly with low watering needs. Credit: Rejden

### 3. Cost Savings

The biggest savings often come from cutting down on watering. A lawn guzzles water, but a well-planned xeriscape may need almost no extra irrigation after it’s established. That means lower water bills and fewer expenses on irrigation systems.

### 4. Environmental Benefits

Working with nature means using fewer chemicals, as native plants naturally resist [local pests](https://www.almanac.com/gardening/pests-and-diseases). Less mowing and watering also means less energy use and fewer carbon emissions—good news for your yard and the planet.

![Image 8: pink yarrow flower clusters in a meadow](https://www.almanac.com/sites/default/files/images/yarrow-pink_full_width.jpg)

My yarrow is not only water-wise but also attracts butterflies, and is excellent for cutting and drying.

10 Principles of Xeriscaping: A Guide to Water-Wise, Low-Maintenance Gardens
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

Here are the tips and tricks that save that precious H₂O while keeping your garden happy and your chores light.

### 1. Pick Plants That Laugh at Drought

If you think xeriscaping means a garden full of prickly cacti and bare rocks, think again! With drought-tolerant plants, native shrubs, and smart planning, you can create a lush, colorful, and low-water landscape that saves both water and time.

Use plants that will thrive with the normal rainfall for your area (e.g., cacti in the Southwest; forsythia, daylilies, and Japanese tree lilacs in the Northeast). Also, seek native plants growing wild in your area; chances are that they survive on only the water that Mother Nature gives them. In addition, they provide habitat for songbirds and other wildlife.

Depending on your location, natives such as beach plum, green ash, mountain laurel, and witch hazel could all be great plants. (Here’s news: Pruning can be all but eliminated if plants are selected based on their ultimate size.) Explore local native nurseries for more.

Here’s a handy list of drought-tolerant plants to get you started. We’ve included herbs native to the hot, dry Mediterranean; sedums and succulents, which hold water in their fat leaves; plants with fuzzy or waxy leaves that are slow to transpire moisture into dry air.

### Drought-Tolerant Plant Picks

| **Perennials, Trees, Shrubs & Groundcovers** | **Succulents & Cacti** | **Drought-Tolerant Annuals** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Russian sage](https://www.almanac.com/plant/russian-sage) | [Sedum/stonecrop](https://www.almanac.com/plant/sedum) (stonecrop) | California poppies |
| California sagebrush | Agave | [Cosmos](https://www.almanac.com/plant/cosmos) |
| [Lavender](https://www.almanac.com/plant/lavender) | Yucca | [Bachelor buttons](https://www.almanac.com/plant/bachelors-buttons) |
| [Daisy](https://www.almanac.com/plant/shasta-daisies) | Opuntia (prickly pear cactus) | Cleome |
| [Black-eyed-susan](https://www.almanac.com/plant/black-eyed-susans) | Various [succulents](https://www.almanac.com/surrender-succulents)! | [Calendula](https://www.almanac.com/plant/how-grow-calendula-complete-guide) |
| [Coneflower](https://www.almanac.com/plant/coneflowers) (Echinacea) |  | Portulaca |
| Blue grama grass |  | Globe amaranth |
| [Ornamental alliums](https://www.almanac.com/growing-allium-ornamental-onions) |  |  |
| [Yarrow](https://www.almanac.com/plant/yarrow) |  |  |
| [Agave](https://www.almanac.com/plant/agave-plant) |  |  |
| [Salvia](https://www.almanac.com/plant/salvia) |  |  |
| [Azalea](https://www.almanac.com/how-grow-azaleas) |  |  |
| Juniper |  |  |
| [Rosemary](https://www.almanac.com/plant/rosemary) |  |  |
| Agastache |  |  |
| [Veronica](https://www.almanac.com/plant/veronica-speedwell) |  |  |
| [Penstemon](https://www.almanac.com/plant/how-plant-grow-and-care-penstemon-beardtongue), or beardtongue |  |  |
![Image 9: Penstemon's showy trumpet-shaped flowers](https://www.almanac.com/sites/default/files/images/penstemon-shutterstock_2490361259_1.jpg)

Penstemon’s love of dry, poor soil makes them a favorite for xeriscapes and anyone tired of babying fussy flowers. Credit: Alex Manders

### 2. Take a Good, Hard Look at Your Lawn

Limit lawn space. Keeping a large expanse of grass looking green in hot, dry weather is very water-intensive! If you must have a lawn, use native grasses or drought-tolerant species.

### 3. Group Plants With Similar Needs

Called zoning, this technique reduces work time by grouping plants with similar watering needs. You can concentrate your watering efforts and give each zone what it needs. Plant the most drought-tolerant plants in your driest areas. If there are some showy, high-maintenance plants that you can’t live without, create an oasis for them in a high-traffic area where you can enjoy them and take care of them easily.

![Image 10: Sedum in my garden](https://www.almanac.com/sites/default/files/images/sedum.jpg)

My sedum thrives on pool soil! Rich soil leads to leggy growth.

### 4. Improve Your Soil

Soil is the secret sauce! Well-aerated soil, rich in organic matter, stores water well yet drains adequately enough to not become soggy. It encourages earthworms and beneficial microorganisms. Plants are able to establish deep roots and [become more resistant to drought](https://www.almanac.com/beat-heat-top-drought-tolerant-plants-resilient-garden) and disease. Happy roots mean plants that stick around through thick and thin.

Just about any soil can benefit from compost; it increases the water-holding capacity of sandy soil and loosens heavy clay soil to improve drainage. Compost is also a storehouse of plant nutrients that are slowly released into the soil to feed the plants over a long period of time. Spread 3 to 4 inches of compost (or well-rotted or bagged manure) over the top of the soil, then turn it in as deeply as you can.

### 5. Water Less

Xeriscaping means having not a waterless landscape but a “water less” one. Use efficient drip irrigation or soaker hoses under mulch to apply water directly to the root zones of plants with similar water needs.

When it’s time to water, soak the soil well, down to about six inches deep, every 10 days or so. This encourages roots to grow deep and strong. [Early morning watering is best](https://www.almanac.com/watering-wisely-drought-or-hot-weather) and, again, water at soil level, not on plant leaves which leads to disease. If you are using overhead sprinklers, water in the evening or early morning to minimize evaporation and wind drift.

### 6. Cover Bare Soil and Mulch!

Cover up bare soil! Mulch is an important weapon against evaporation and provides a cushion during a downpour to lessen erosion and give the rain a chance to soak in rather than running off. It will also keep weeds from competing with your plants for precious moisture.

As the mulch breaks down, it releases nutrients into the soil, providing natural fertilization. Compost and finely shredded bark are excellent mulch choices. Apply a 2- to 3-inch layer throughout the planting bed and replenish as needed.

**Tip**: [Use our mulch calculator](https://www.almanac.com/tool/mulch-calculator) to estimate how much you need.

### 7. Got Hills or Uneven Ground? Keep Water Where It Counts

If your ground is sloped or hilly, water can quickly run off before your plants get a drink. Grading is important. To slow it down, try creating terraces—flat steps cut into the hillside—that help keep soil and water from washing away.

You can also build small raised rings of soil called berms around your plants. These act like little bowls that catch and hold water right where roots need it most.

Another easy trick is to dig shallow channels or ditches that guide rainwater toward thirsty plants instead of letting it rush away.

### 8. Lay Off the Fertilizer

Too much fertilizer is like feeding your plants dessert every day—it makes them grow fast but wanting even more water. Plus, the don’t grow up strong and are more susceptible to attack by insects and disease. Fertilize sparingly, if at all.

### 9. Re-Evaluate the Lawn and Use Ground Cover

Limit lawn space. Keeping turfgrass looking green in hot, dry weather is very water-intensive. Plus, a lawn takes more fertilization, pest control, and mowing than any other part of the landscape. If you walk on the grass only to mow it, consider an alternative.

Ground covers are low plants that grow together to form living mulch that crowds out weeds, prevents erosion, and never needs mowing. Like a lawn, a ground cover unites different landscape areas.

*   Sun-loving ground covers such as blue rug junipers, bearberry, bugleweed, and creeping phlox are perfect for steep slopes and open areas.
*   For shade, choose pachysandra, wild ginger, and sweet woodruff. Or, install stone pavers. Leave gaps between them for low plants such as thyme or Irish moss.

### 10. Give Gray Water a Thought

That “used” water from your sinks and laundry—called gray water—can get a second life watering shrubs, fruit trees, and flowers. But use it carefully; it’s not for veggie patches and can sometimes contain chemicals best kept away from delicate plants.

Ready to Dig In? Tips for Starting Your Xeriscape
-------------------------------------------------

Ask yourself (and maybe the family,too):

*   Are we really using this lawn, or is it just thirsty and high-maintenance?
*   How much water does it swallow every week?
*   Would we miss it—or would we rather have something easier and prettier to care for?

If the answer leans toward letting it go, you’re not alone. Lawns often take more than they give, especially in dry climates.

1.   For smaller yards, removing turf can be a doable weekend project with a shovel, garden fork, and a bit of elbow grease.
2.   Or try sheet mulching: lay down cardboard, top it with mulch, and let nature do the hard work of smothering the grass and enriching the soil beneath.
3.   Once the lawn is cleared, pause to plan…

Xeriscape Planning and Design
-----------------------------

1.   Sketch an overhead view of your property on graph paper or list the [types of planting areas in your yard](https://www.almanac.com/landscape-design-ideas-and-advice-beginners).
2.   Note compass direction and areas of sun and shade.
3.   Locate and consider your microclimates: Are there dry spots where only drought-tolerant plants should be used? Do you have shaded areas where the soil is usually moist? Got any wetter places that might never need irrigation?
4.   Do existing trees or buildings shelter plants from drying winds and intense sun? Could a steep slope become a terraced bed to catch rainwater runoff?
5.   Decide where to place rocks, mulch, and water-wise plants.
6.   Again, group plants with similar needs together, and shape the land so rainwater flows where it’s needed most.

Not sure what grows best in your region? Reach out to a local garden center or your county extension office for expert advice.

After planting, your xeriscape won’t need much: occasional deep watering, a little pruning, and basic weeding. It’s not no-maintenance, but it’s a whole lot less than mowing and watering a lawn.

![Image 11: A Xeriscape garden filled with flowering Golden Yarrow plants of various sizes and Purple Larkspur plants.](https://www.almanac.com/sites/default/files/images/xeriscape-shutterstock_2275546237.jpg)

A xeriscape in full bloom—golden yarrow and purple larkspur prove that low-water gardening can dazzle with color! Credit: Susan Hodgson

### Cold-Zone Considerations

Xeriscaping in colder climates is an option as well. You don’t need to live in a hot and dry location to enjoy its benefits. Hardy plants for Xeriscaping in northern zones (2, 3, or 4)include:

**Perennials**

*   Daylily (_Hemerocallis_), Zones 2 and 3 Delphinium (_Delphinium_), Zone 3
*   Purple coneflower (_Echinacea purpurea_), Zone 3

**Trees**

*   Ash-leaved maple/box elder (_Acer negundo_), Zone 2
*   Eastern white pine _(Pinus strobus_), Zone 3
*   European bird cherry (_Prunus padus_), Zone 3

**Shrubs**

*   Common lilac (_Syringa vulgaris_), Zone 3
*   Smooth sumac _(Rhus glabra_), Zone 2
*   Siberian pea tree (_Caragana arborescens_), Zone 2

**Ground Covers**

*   Bugleweed (_Ajuga_), Zone 3
*   Common bearberry (_Arctostaphylos uva-ursi_), Zone 2
*   Lily-of-the-valley _(Convallaria majalis_), Zone 2

_**Note**_: Some of the plants listed above are considered invasive in certain areas. Before planting them, it’s best to consult your local Cooperative Extension or garden center to determine their suitability.

### Xeric Plant Combos

For a foundation planting, try colorful groupings of water-thrifty ‘Blue Star’ juniper, deep maroon ‘Diabolo’ ninebark, purple smoke bush, and a rainbow mix of summer azaleas. For a perennial border that brings late-summer bloom and needs minimal watering, try a mix of ‘Autumn Joy’ sedum, silvery catmint with spiky blue flowers, magenta flower poppy mallow, and several varieties of asters. Other options: Most rock garden plants, many ornamental grasses, and numerous herbs can add interest without requiring a lot of work.

### How Some Plants Fight Back

![Image 12: dusty miller in the garden](https://www.almanac.com/sites/default/files/images/dusty%20miller%20Joyce%20Grace%20GettyImages.jpg)

It may look cool in color, but it’s a sun lover which thrives in heat! Photo credit: Joyce Grace/Getty Images

Often the qualities that make plants heat- and drought-resistant are the very same ones that make them attractive and/or interesting.

*   The silver foliage of dusty miller and silver mound artemisia and grayish leaves of lavender reflect sunlight and thus resist withering.
*   Sedums, hens and chicks, and other succulents store water in their thick, fleshy roots and stems.
*   Junipers, white spruce, and other evergreens with small needles leave little surface area exposed to the sun and hence are less likely to succumb to its drying effects.
*   Plants such as bayberry and vinca have waxy leaf surfaces that resist drying winds.

### The Bottom Line

Xeriscaping isn’t just a good idea—it’s an achievable one. With a little planning and a solid understanding of your local climate, you can grow a garden that’s both beautiful and water-wise.

Native plants (and well-behaved non-natives) do the heavy lifting—saving water, reducing work, and thriving without fuss. And with all the colors, textures, and shapes to choose from, your landscape can still look lush, inviting, and full of life.

Work with nature, not against it—and you’ll have a garden that gives more than it takes.

_**Have you tried xeriscaping? What tips, plants, or lessons would you share with fellow gardeners? Let us know in the comments!**_

enriched_points.expanded_content: Xeriscaping emphasizes water conservation through strategic plant selection, soil management, and efficient irrigation methods, creating sustainable landscapes that thrive with minimal water.
Choosing native, drought-tolerant plants reduces water needs significantly, ensuring that landscapes remain vibrant and sustainable even in arid climates.
Enhancing soil with organic matter like compost improves water retention and drainage, promoting healthier plant growth and resilience against drought conditions.

enriched_points.point_title: Use of Mulch for Moisture Retention
enriched_points.expanded_content: Applying mulch conserves soil moisture, suppresses weeds, and regulates temperature, effectively reducing the need for frequent watering and maintenance.
Zoning plants based on their water requirements optimizes irrigation efficiency, ensuring each plant receives adequate moisture while conserving overall water usage.
